md: The two axes divide the coordinate plane into **four regions**, called **quadrants**. Each one has a Roman numeral name: I, II, III, IV. They're numbered starting from the top right and going *counterclockwise*.
Pick any spot — the readout will tell you which quadrant (or which axis) the point is on.
**Quadrant II** (top left): $x < 0$, $y > 0$ — x negative, y positive.
**Quadrant IV** (bottom right): $x > 0$, $y < 0$ — x positive, y negative.

blocks.title: How to remember the order
blocks.body: Start at the top right and go around the way a hand sweeps a clock *backwards* (counterclockwise). I, II, III, IV. The numbering is the same in every textbook everywhere.

md: Some points don't belong to any quadrant — they sit *on* an axis.
**The origin**: $x = 0$ and $y = 0$ — it's where all four quadrants meet.
Given $(x, y)$, you only need the **signs**:

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: Four quadrants, numbered counterclockwise from the top right. Use the sign of $x$ and $y$ to identify the quadrant in seconds. Points with a zero coordinate sit on an axis, not in a quadrant.
